title: vimeo_1185709694
displayTitle: 2026-04-22 - Health Committee - Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of the Mental Health and Wellbeing Commission
topic: Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of the Mental Health and Wellbeing Commission
description: Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of the Mental Health and Wellbeing Commission Mental Health and Wellbeing Commission Karen Orsborn, Chief Executive Hayden Wano, Board Chair Dr Barbara Disley, Deputy Board Chair
descriptionSummary: Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of the Mental Health and Wellbeing Commission. Mental Health and Wellbeing Commission. Karen Orsborn, Chief Executive

chunks.text: Good morning- Good morning... everybody. It's very good to have you here. Karen Osborne, Hayden Waino and Dr. Barbara Disley. I'll let you introduce yourselves as well, but I understand we have you here for two hours today. I'll let you open up with some introductory remarks after introduction, and then I'll have the two members to ask questions. Over to you.

Thank you for making the time. Thank you. Tēnā koe, e te tiamana, koutou nga mangai o te komiti nei. Me ana ra i roto nga tini āhuatanga o te wā. Thank you again for the opportunity to allow us to present our last year's work and perhaps a look into what we're looking into in the future.

As many of you will know that we were set up following the inquiry into mental health and addiction back in 2018, and we came into form in February '21. So, six years of existence. At the time, the commission was set up to be a force for change, helping to lead a shift to a mental health wellbeing system that is accountable and is experienced as joined up, respectful, responsive by tāngata whai ora, their families, and whānau.

The system is serving a population now that is poorer in mental health and wellbeing, an increasing need for mental health support compared to six years ago. There have been improvements. More staff in place due to filling vacancies has been a key driver to improve access rates and reduce wait times.

Over 236,000 people have seen the Access and Choice program in 2024, but less than the expected target of 325,000 per annum. However, for young people, access to specialist mental health services reduced. They faced longer wait times, had more referrals declined.

This is particularly concerning given the rate of psychological distress that is increasing for young people. In 2024 and '25 was the coming of age for Te Hiranga Mahara. Our system performance monitor showed a system view linking outcomes for people with contributions from the government.

Our first system performance monitoring report outlined what a good mental health and addiction system looks like. The report shows that while there are pockets of positive change, there is a significant way to go to achieve the outcomes people expect. Our annual stakeholder engagement survey told us there is an overall positive perception of Te Hiranga Mahara.

Stakeholders rated us highly on our engagement, reliability of advice, reports, and information that we publish, and speaking up on important mental health, addiction, and wellbeing issues. Māori, iwi, and lived experience stakeholders report positive engagement with us.

We can see the impact of our work in three key areas: the Access and Choice program, the work to repeal and replace the Mental Health Act, and the new Mental Health and Wellbeing Strategy. The launch of the Access and Choice program represented a major investment in mental health and addiction.

Our monitoring has provided transparency and accountability. In response to our findings, we saw greater investment in targeted action and improvement in data quality and visibility. Since our establishment, we've advocated for a mental health and addiction system that places people and whānau at the center.

Central to this has been our support for the work to repeal and replace the Mental Health Act. We support legislative and practice changes that uphold people's rights to make informed decisions about their care and treatment. We've advocated for stronger recognition of mental health and wellbeing within legislation.

Our evidence-based advice made the case for a dedicated mental health and wellbeing strategy anchored in research and shaped by the voices of people with lived experience. Our engagement has been acknowledged and actively sought with the Minister for Mental Health supporting our ongoing involvement in the development of the Mental Health and Wellbeing Strategy.

In the coming year ahead, as our 25/26 is our first year under our new strategy and statement of intent. We have new strategic priorities focused on mental health and addiction services for tāngata whai ora and outcomes for people with lived experience, and on improving the performance of the mental health and addiction system.

We're focusing our efforts over the next three years through making three strategic shifts on how we work, placing people with lived experience at the center of our work, being future-oriented, promoting alignment and leveraging knowledge to influence positive change, and being a bold advocate.

The first Hauora Heringa Ro Te Ara Tūroa forum was a success with over 240 attendees. Feedback was positive, particularly about the timely announcement regarding crisis response funding. Most delegates surveyed found the program relevant, topical and useful.

The publication of Urupare Morerere Crisis Response Monitoring Report was a significant milestoneAnalysis of media cover showed 23 articles across major outlets in an estimated reach of 2.8 million impressions. We published the assessment of progress implementation of Ko Tima Te Haringa recommendations.

This is the first time that we have assessed progress with recommendations that we've made. Of the five recommendations made in early 2024, three have been completed by Health New Zealand and the government. Two others are being worked on. This year, in 2026, the Minister for Mental Health will undertake a review of our effectiveness that was built into the legislation from the beginning and the operations of Te Hiringa Māhara.
Our case studies highlight the system is shifting.

We are a trusted voice in speaking up on important health and mental health, addiction, and wellbeing issues. We recognize much more needs to be done and will take time, sustained leadership, and action. The new mental health and wellbeing strategy and our ongoing oversight, monitoring, and advocacy will be critical to seeing the change people and whanau want to see. Tena koutou.

Thank you. Thanks very much for that. Some questions. Ingrid Lari. Yeah. Thank you very much, and lovely to see you all. I'm interested to pick up some of the themes that you mentioned in your introduction, and clear that, when you say a trusted voice, that certainly is the perceptions in the sector.

So I'm curious to know some things from a systems perspective that your commission has highlighted. One of the things we asked you about a number of years ago was leadership in the mental health sector, and I'm wanting to get a sense from you whether you feel that the leadership is there. And I'm thinking also about accountability, given that we have more agencies who are supposed to or have a statutory duty to provide some accountability.

But when we ask them all, fingers go the other way, or there just isn't the legislative teeth. And in fact, when I look, I see accountability's only really at a contracted level, which are very output-orientated. So there's two systems levels questions there. One is, is the leadership there? Secondly, are you satisfied that the accountabilities are baked into the system in the way they should be around mental health?

Let me start. I think we can all probably have a bit of a go at that question. It's a big one, Ingrid. So, in terms of leadership, we've seen some change and improvement, particularly within Health New Zealand. And we've been able to actively engage at the governance and senior operational level. So we've seen a deficit, I suppose, of leadership in that space that has been being filled in recent times.

In terms of accountability, I think there's still ways to go. We think that the mental health and wellbeing strategy is an important one, that it has an overlay that includes within it milestones and accountabilities that you refer to. I can give one example of where system change leadership where we particularly played a role, and that's in the crisis response space.

There was a lack of clear leadership across the various different agencies, police, justice, and health, and we managed to fill a void. And I think that as a result of that, there was some progress made, and then government subsequently making some investments. So, change is happening in pockets, but I would still say in terms of overall accountability in a joined up system, we have a long way to go. And as I said in that introduction, it's going to require time and sustained leadership for us to get there.

That's a starter. Or Karen. Could I sleep on that, Mr. Chair? Of course you can. Thank you for that. And I guess that leads me into a pivot, if you like, or a focus now on youth. And I know that in your latest report, we're still seeing youth having longer wait times to access specialist services.

And in fact, we're seeing longer wait times for addiction services as well. So I'm interested to know whether the focus on youth is-- whether you're able, within the current architecture, to include some of the really important things like social determinants and other elements that might make a significant difference to lifting that access and engagement.
I think there's two parts to your question. In terms of the first part of your question...

Yes. Sure thing. Just sorry, can you clarify that first part? So the first part is around the wait times for- Yes. Sorry. Wait times... 19 to 24-year-olds. So if we're unpicking it, I think is probably the best one-line statement we can say. What we're finding in for young people is the referrals are being made, but they're being declined, and so understanding what that's about is important to us.

Is that work happening? Do you know the tracking of the declines? Karen. Yeah. Okay, I can comment on that. So we've had a long-term focus on rangatahi and young people, looking at both what are the drivers for the increasing rates of distress, in that sort of determinant space. And we reported on the wellbeing outcomes for rangatahi and youth, as well as access to services. So both is really important.

You've seen youth reflected in our crisis and other work, and we are doing a deep dive this year to really try and understand those pathways across the system, but also the access patterns. So what we have seen is, as Hayden said, a continued decline in the numbers of young people accessing specialist services. And there are more options in primary and community, but we're still hearing a lot about the concerns that people have about young people not being able to get access when they need it.

So we need to understand how that whole system's working. Yeah, I'll just leave it there. She's on, she- Yeah. Oh, yeah. But I want a sup on the second round. Thank you. Before a sup, and then it's Kahu. Yeah, just final sup. Thank you for that. Last night when I asked the minister about this, he said that people are being referred to different organizations or different specialists, and that's why there are these declines.

Have you seen evidence that young people are actually being deferred into services and receiving the treatment, or is that a statement that needs some evidence behind it? Yeah. Look, we have heard that referrals are being declined because there's more appropriate services, and we know there are more options in those primary and community services, particularly like the youth stream of access and choice and other options.

We don't fully understand the detail that sits behind that, which is what we're hoping to understand more in the deep dive that we're doing this year. So there is something happening in terms of that 18 to 24 age group who are accessing adult services. So wanting to understand what's happening within services and why young people, that age group, has continued to decline while adults has increased. So there is something under there that we need to understand, so we're looking at it.

Which is what my sup was going to be about, is that transition from youth to adult and being able to understand what is the gap or the blockage in that transition. If you have any comment on that. Yeah. Look, we haven't particularly looked at that transition, and so that is part of the work we're doing this year, as part of the deep dive, looking at young people and how that transition works. Because we have heard many...

Young people are all different, and so for some, they may be more ready to transition into adult services. Others might prefer to actually be continued to be supported in the services they're currently receiving. So as I said, that's what we're looking at this year. Right. So you're going to look at that to understand what that blockage or- Yes.

Mm. Yes. And so in our work, what we do is we have the broad brush approach to monitoring, and then we highlight the areas where we need to have a closer look. And last year, it was really clear that crisis responses was an area we needed to have a closer look. So we did that deep dive last year. This year, it's really having a look at those youth services.

And that's how we work as an entity, is to say, "Where do we need to have a closer look?" And do that work. So that there's a gap in that transition bit at the moment. Yeah. That's what we need to understand more. Yeah. And that's where we add as value to the system, is being able to look at those deep dives, bring some international evidence, what is working well in New Zealand, what can we learn across the system. So that's what we're doing.

I- Thank you, Mr. Chair, and thank you for being here and for your mahi. We know that our rangatahi have some of the worst statistics for mental health in the OECD, and I want to talk about your advocacy role, particularly with other ministries and ministers. So we know that the new Minister Stanford's new curriculum, particularly the health and physical education curriculum, does not mention the words mental health at all.

How have you advocated to the minister or the Ministry for Education to have mental health within the curriculum? Yeah. We haven't had any specific advocacy to the minister. When we have those relationships across government, and certainly we know that other government entities, agencies, draw from our work, and so we certainly advocate for youth voice, so for young people to have a voice, in whatever changes are being proposed.

But also for young people to have greater knowledge about mental health and wellbeing, what they can do to keep themselves well and safe. So yeah, we certainly advocate for that. Thank you. Supplementary. And so do you think it is vital for youth and rangatahi to get support, information, and understanding through their education at school?

Yeah. Look, this is a personal response. Yes. Have we done any particular work in a specific area? We haven't. And supplementary. And so has the Ministry for Education or the Minister of Education reached out to you for that advice? I'm not aware of that. Thank you. And one more, if that's okay.

It's okay. Thank you. So polite. Has there been any discussion around or thoughts around having a cross-ministry group that meets, like the group that was set up after Te Aorerekura for the prevention of family and sexual violence was set up, where they came together, talked about mental health, particularly around youth, for this case. Has there been any work or thoughts done around that?

With the focus on education and wellbeing? With a focus on youth mental health. Not that I'm aware of. Yeah. Do you think that- We've- Yeah. The closest piece of work that we've been involved in there would be in the crisis response space. Mm. And there have been some short-term ministry-led initiatives, but not specifically on this kaupapa.

Mm. There has been work across government in previous terms of government with drawing together different ministers with different portfolios to focus on the child and youth wellbeing. Yeah. Thank you. Hamish. Yes, sir. Bring it. Thank you.

During the year, you've updated your strategy. What is the main shifts in your priorities, and what are they based on? Good question. I think a number of factors came to play. I'll start the ball rolling, but a number of factors came to play for us. One, we found our straps, I suppose you could say, being a startup 60 years ago. So we were clear about what our purpose was, and we were clear about the resources that we had.

The other part, of course, is that we utilized the public service to assist us in engaging with stakeholders. So we listened to a lot of people, in effect, and helped shape our strategy. So knowing what we were about, knowing the resources that we had, knowing what we heard, and that brought us into identifying six areas to shift where from to.

And I think I'm going to ask Karen just to cover off on those three areas, but they're quite important for us in terms of driving the decisions that we make at the board table and elsewhere inside the organization. So last year, we did undertake what's called a future excellence horizon, which is common across government. So we interviewed a range of stakeholders and also conducted a survey to get feedback from people about if we were the best organization we could be, what that would look like.

And so what we heard was that we needed to place people's lived experience much more centered in our work. We needed to work to improve a fragmented system. So we'd heard a lot about the fragmentation and the lack of leadership. We needed to lift our profile, so we heard quite a lot about our visibility, and that we needed to look ahead and think about the demographic changes that were happening. And so that then led into the refocus of our strategy, and our 24/25 annual report reports against our previous four strategic priorities.

So what we're now working to is a refined set of strategic priorities, which is about focusing on people with lived experience, people who are using mental health and addiction services, so much more focused on services. Continuing to have a focus on broader wellbeing outcomes and determinants. And the new one is really how we look at the performance of the mental health and addiction system.

And, as we've mentioned previously, we did release our first system performance report last year. We are about to release our second one this year in June, and that is where the six shifts that we identified. So we said that the system needs to shift in these ways, and that's really informed the mental health and wellbeing strategy.

So, happy to take further questions on that. Just a quick set. In multiple of your answers, you've talked about your stakeholders. Who do you see your stakeholders? Who do you see those people or stakeholders? Who are they? Oh, look, they're really broad. We have multiple stakeholders. So of course, it's people who have lived experience and the whanau, families who support them, and that's a really broad and diverse group.

We have obligations to Maori and whanau as part of our legislation and how we engage with Maori and whanau and prioritize equity for Maori. We have some really important sector stakeholders, so working with people who are providing services, commissioning services to achieve the changes that we want to see across government. We also have stakeholders in terms of what we describe as the priority populations.

So in our act, there are a list of 12 populations who have a higher prevalence of distress, and so it's important that we connect with those groups as well. So Maori, Pacific, young people, veterans is the list. So it's a really broad group, and we're a small entity of around 20 staff. So we have to really think smartly about how we connect in with organizations that represent those populations and with the providers, so both Health New Zealand and NGOs, other providers. So it's a big challenge for us, how we keep that

connection. I appreciate you've got your priority populations, and I think last year when you were here, I did ask about middle-aged males. Yes. They're the group that are most likely to die by suicide. What are you doing to reach out to stakeholders in that group?

Yeah. So- Because I suppose one of my fears is that some of these people aren't accessing mental health support. Yeah. And so probably very hard for you to pick up because they're not engaging with services. Yeah. And look, I think that's the relationships we have with other agencies and organizations who are connected with those groups. Because it's hard for us to have the direct reach into those communities, given the nature of our organization and our size. So we do have that intentional engagement with the groups that represent,

so be it the rural sector or if it's Pacific peoples. So how do we have those connections? And then also more broadly, like the Mental Health Foundation and those organizations. Yes. And just one quick follow-up. Hayden, during your initial briefing, you mentioned that case studies show that the system is shifting. Is that in relation to these priorities, or were you meaning something different?

I was referring to the areas that we've seen in the legislation, the taking up of the strategy for mental health and wellbeing, focus on youth, and we're also talking about access and choice. So those are the areas that we particularlyWhere we've worked on, where we can see some contribution to where those areas of work have shifted.

A supp on that. Definitely. True. Move a supp on that. Yeah. Just to clarify, the system shift you're talking about are mainly inputs in terms of legislative change and systems change rather than outcomes, which would take some time to measure. Of course. Thank you.

Yes. Quite right. Yeah. Right. Carlos. The supp or primary? Supplementary. Why do you have a- Yes... supplementary? I've got you down as a primary. You're after Carlos. You're next. Carlos. Thank you, Chair. I read your statement of intent that was published in last year, July.

Mm-hmm. But I couldn't find anything that talk about the needs of the AFEX community. In the past five years, the risk of mental health issues like depression have been significantly increased in Asian communities. Mm-hmm. Do you think this is a serious concern that need more attention?

Mm-hmm. Yes, we do. We're not reflecting necessarily all of that work that's been done, but there's some work that's been done through Karen and the team, and that will, in time, surface. But perhaps Karen's in a better position to articulate the work that we're doing.

Yes. I mean, if you think it is a very serious concern, why not a single wording or single strategies have been addressing the needs of our Asian community? It probably reflects the fact that we weren't there at that particular time. But it's not to say... It's basically saying we can't get to everything at once.

But having said that, we're now in a position, and we are starting to move into making connections in that particular area. So you're trying to tell me that you don't think there's a needs for our Asian community before? We knew that. You knew, but you didn't react. Well, again, a small organization trying to do the best that we can in terms of reaching.

If you look at all 12 population groups, they're quite extensive and require quite a lot of effort and resource to engage in an appropriate way and follow through. So, at that particular time, we clearly weren't in a position to articulate anything. We are in a better position to do that now.

All right. So what do you think are the main reason affecting the mental health in our AFEX community? How does the Mental Health and Wellbeing Commission support a speaker for them? So, I've had a lot of engagement, particularly with Asian Family Services, and so we have supported the summits that Kelly and her team have hosted, both in Auckland and then the one in Parliament. And we do look at how we reflect Asian communities, Asian population in our substantive reports. So for example, in our access and choice report, we

highlighted the needs for Asian people to have access to those access and choice services. And in our other reports, we also highlight what are the needs for Asian people alongside Maori and Pacific. So we certainly have done work to connect with both Asian Family Services and other key leaders from the Asian community, and to reflect that in our work.

Mr Chair, have you removed the barrier for the Asian community to access to mental health services? Have we removed the barrier? Any barrier. Oh, I think- Or you just believe that there's no barrier for any AFEX groups or Asian communities to access to the mental health service?

Our role is to highlight what's happening in terms of access and need, and then to advocate for that. And in terms of for the Asian population, we have been reflecting those needs and doing that advocacy in our work. And I think this does highlight the challenge, as Hayden said.

There are 12 priority populations, and it's hard to reflect each of those populations specifically, particularly in a statement of intent, which is around us as an entity. Mr Chair. One more. But in our substantive work, we certainly do everything to bring forward those voices in our work. If you just mentioned that your main function is to advocate for people or give advices, what advice have you given to the minister so far to address the needs of our Asian communities? And also, are you happy with the result you have been implementing the last 12 months so far?

If not, which areas do you think it needs improve? We have raised the issue with the minister. We haven't got a clear pathway forward, but we have raised it as an important area that needs attention. And specifically in relation to the access and choice program and their report at the end of five years.

And we did highlight the need for a service stream that was specific for the Asian population. Just a quick comment. And the minister responded to that in a positive way. Just a last comment. Hold on, Carlos. Yeah, just a quick comment, yeah. You have a quick comment, and then I'm going to Ingrid for a supp, and Cam also has a supp. Just let you know I'm very dis- No, hold on, Carlos. And then Kahu has her primary.

Oh, sorry. It was a supplementary, not a primary. Thank you. Quick comment. Just let you know, I'm very disappointed with the answer you provide today, and I expect a better outcome in the next 12 months. Thank you. Thank you. Supplementary. Yeah. Ingrid Leary. Thank you for that. Speaking to Dr. Kelly Feng, it seems that one of the problems is that you don't know what you don't know when it comes to the Asian- But mental health issues and gambling in particular.

So first, is that a piece of work that is-- when you talk about the work that you're doing, Karen, is that what you're looking at? Because that baseline appears to be missing, and that's probably a big part of why they don't get funding that I think Dr Chung is alluding to is not proportionate to the problem. We can't count it. The second part is with access and choice.

Are you aware that the access and choice solution provided by the government is not the same as the other strands of access and choice, is convoluted, and therefore makes it really problematic for Asian communities to actually access access and choice? And if so, what would you like to see done about that? Yeah.

So just on that second part, I am aware that the solution for the Asian stream of access and choice is different from the other streams, and that reflects that those other streams were right from the beginning, and then this is a solution to improve access. And I know that's, I haven't talked with Kelly recently, but some aspects of that are quite challenging, and those do need to be worked through.

I think ultimately it is about people being able to access the services and supports in a timely way. So it is about the specific streams, and it is also about the appropriateness of all services for people who are accessing those services. The Asian population is made up of different types of population, as it is for Pacific peoples as well. So it is how do we, yeah, as said, have the specific streams and services that meet everybody's needs in the most appropriate way.

And then, so is it baselining of the how big is the problem, which is the question, and is difficult if the data is only being collected from small data sets of who is accessing- Yes... and what we're seeing on the ground is a much bigger problem. Is that a gap in the system, and is that something that you are looking at, or? Yeah, and that goes to the prevalence study. Mm-hmm.

So we have advocated for a proper sort of study of the level of need in communities. And we're really pleased- Speaking particularly about the Asian community. Yes. Well, that would include what's the level of need for Asian communities as well as other communities. So the youth stream is underway, and that should give us a really good insight into what is the level of need for all of those populations, and then how can services be targeted to meet those needs. And particularly for the youth does pick up, that's part of those changing demographics.

So, you see that particularly in that age group. So that will give us some really useful information. But not only us, for the whole system. So we have a role to play- Mm-hmm... but we're not the commissioners and funders of services. So we're bringing that oversight. Yeah. So, we have views and insights, and we can feed those in with.

I think you can say that we are increasing our presence in this area, and you will see more from us. Thank you. Karen. Oh, thank you, Chair. Supplementary. Cheers. I heard there's a supplementary then you ask for your primary. The questions that we've had in this line of question, we have focused on groups of people, specifically. Heard some strong advocacy and questions from Dr Chung on Asian, Dr Campbell on middle-aged men. Do you think that labeling, listing, categorizing the way it's done in this act, in your enabling piece of legislation, is actually

making your job harder when a population is divided up and everyone starts fighting from ? Do you think that it's actually the amount of trust in what you're able to do is being impacted because of the dividing people up and clear breach of a social contract where we all come together to support each other?

Or do you think politicians are dividing us? Yeah, look, I think- Like, is it making your job- I think it's-... easier or harder? I think it's helpful- Speaking to the-... to be-... base I think if we're looking at it from a general how we apply out the resources that we have, it gives us, it recognizes that not one shoe or one solution fits all.

But it does provide a challenge for us when it comes to applying that, and practically, there are some challenges for us to be able to move effectively across all of those bases. Is that because there's contradictions in the act between things like, I think it's Section 10, where you can, as a commission, can decide populations you want to focus on, but then also you've got Clause 13, Schedule 2 with these lists of populations you have to justify, actually looking at wider population bases.

I guess I'm trying to figure out, like, we've heard some emotion. I would say there's a lot of people who would read this legislation, and yeah, you could say it's a politician, and say, are you actually able to look at the population and identify, based on what you find or do you feel directed by this to these population bases?

Mm-hmm. If I could add a comment, because it is one of the shifts that we've made in our strategy. So when we started in the early period of time, we did do a report focused on the wellbeing outcomes for Pacific peoples, to focus on a particular population group. Now, that report was incredibly well-received by the Pacific community. But when we've looked at our resources, and as I said, we're $5 million and 20 staff, and what we went through with the future excellence horizon, the advice was that we needed to be much more focused on people with lived experience and

people who are accessing services And so we have made a shift to, and it was a challenging shift for us to make, because we would like to be able to do a report like that on each of the priority populations, but we simply don't have the resources to enable us to do that.

So we have focused on the people who are accessing services, who are using services, and the people with distress, and trying to bring the voices of those different priority populations into that work. So it is hard to disaggregate because it's not that people can be categorized into more than one of those categories. So it's not just a straight distinction.

So it is a challenge for us that we've had to grapple with. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Well, we know too many Asian people in Aotearoa don't feel welcome and have ill mental health. And my colleague from the National Party, Dr. Carlos Cheng, talked about the need for culturally appropriate services and getting support to populations who are disproportionately affected.

For example, people living in rural areas, like farmers. Mm-hmm. When we're talking about Asian family services, it could look from the outside like they get quite a lot of funding compared to other services. But we know that that funding is ring-fenced for gambling, and that means that other mental health services, which maybe are more needed in different areas, are not able to be funded because it is ring-fenced for gambling.

And so my question is around that access and peer support, and what are your suggestions for more flexible contracts and reactive contracts for the need? What's your advice to funders of mental health services? Yeah, look, generally, we see a lot of our advice that we provide is aimed at policymakers and decision-makers, and we think the levers of commissioning are really important levers for change. So we don't have our hand directly on those levers, but what

we can do is shine a light by bringing information to bear so that hopefully that influences in a positive way. And I think that's important that we're not seen as the fixers of issues, because that's just not what we're designed to do. So, commissioning is a really important area for change, as I've said, and I think the kinds of things that you're looking for usually, particularly if you want community-based responses, is high trust, flexibility in tenure or term, being clear on the expectation and clear on the outcome that you're looking for,

really clear steps and measures that are put in place, and allowing that community to determine the best way. So, using that tight-loose type principle, being clear on what you're looking for, being clear on what you're measuring, and allowing that community to shape what the response needs to be. Can I just make another comment there?

That in terms of the way we are looking at data or the systems looking at data, in terms of being clearer about the impact across different ethnicities, age groups, other factors that might play into this space, that's really important to allow us to be able to look down into those various variables about what are the presenting needs, what's the difference being made, what are the things that would make a difference for particular populations or age groups, and what might we be doing about it in commissioning space.

And I guess what we're seeing is a higher level of focus on some of those different categories, which may not have been there in the past, but does allow greater information. And so as access and choice and those community programs roll out, we're in a better position now to see who's getting access and who isn't, and what might need to be done about that.

And I think that's the area we'll be in in the next few years. Thank you, Mr. Chair. I'd like to ask some questions which are intended to get from you a model for me to better understand your and other mental health data in the future. Mm-hmm.

I notice that your letter of expectation includes analysis of current resourcing against population needs to inform future areas of focus at a system level. Often, when we talk about health service planning, we have a conceptual model of how we might measure need in the community, and therefore the service response.

When we read reports like the one you recently published, 22.9% of people aged 15 to 24 experience high or very high levels of psychological distress. Can you please explain the conceptual model that sits behind that measurement? Or tell me another measurement, but the measurement of need and what the services could or should be. How do you link that?

Do you want to- Look, yeah So what we've used in terms of that 22.9% is using the health survey results. So that, as you know, has limitations. So that's been used as a proxy to say what's the best information we have about the level of need. But the prevalence study will give us far better information about the actual level of need and prevalence of- Okay. I don't want to get into a discussion about, Mr.

Deputy Chair, if I may. I don't want to get a discussion about measurement and methods. What I'm asking is, whatever good measurement you use- Yeah... how will you match that or link it to service need? Does every person who reports experiencing distress need a clinical service or not? And can you explain that link to me?

So what I would say is our work is, in terms of that system performance view, and I'll refer back to the system performance work, is we had our first report last year, and it might not be the one that you're referring to, but we're trying to say what are the population level outcomes for the well-being for all people in Aotearoa, and how do you then draw connections back into the system and into the service contribution? And there's not that direct connection through. But what we're trying, in terms of attribution, there's many other factors that sit

outside of the mental health system, financial crisis, wars, all of those things actually influence people's mental health and wellbeing. So we're trying to draw that connection. But the report from last year was year one. And that work will develop over time, but it's trying to develop those insights and knowledge about where we need to do more work and to start to paint the picture.

So I think I understand where you're trying to go with that, but we've got a long way to get to before we can really say what is the level of need and what is the appropriate response, be it in an all of government type approach in terms of improving education, employment- Support services... or is it about what are the services, because it's not everybody needs a service.

Yeah. In that way, it could be more preventative actions, some of the wellbeing service supports that can be provided. Interesting. So that's the complexity of what we're trying to understand. So let's stay on the service provision side because that is an issue that, as members of Parliament and yourselves, will hear all the time from people who are struggling to access services. So certainly there's a desire in all parts of the system, a recognition that the level of services need to be increased. But are you saying

that we don't yet know the desirable level of service in response to these need measures in New Zealand or the whole world? Because I have an idea of the level of obstetric services that New Zealand women need, and we could link that to the fertility rate or whatever- Yes... and therefore plan health services around them.

Similar with this committee has gone through a process with aged care. So that conceptual model of how to link measures of need to services doesn't ex... Can you help me understand? Because it seems like a major problem for planning health services. I would agree with you, and I think that's really why we've been really pushing as an advocate for the epi studies.

And if you look back, youth has never been identified in any of our, as far as I'm aware. Okay. We haven't done anything since 2006 to update what actual need is. And so I think our view is that commissioners and planners are flying in the blind, and so information and good quality and timely information is an issue.

Well, having my background in epidemiology, I'm not going to argue against a study being done. Yeah. But when a study is done- Mm-hmm... you then need to decide what to do with the result. Yep. And let's say you find something similar to this 22.9% of the 15 to 24-year-olds, which by the way came from your- Mm-hmm... strategy, figure three in your strategy, experience distress. So we might well get a number that's better or worse in some subgroups for- Mm-hmm... their level of mental distress and- Mm-hmm... no matter how good our epi study, we still wouldn't know what to do if we don't

have a model that says all of these people need to see a specialist or- Mm-hmm... these people need access, community-based services. What it's- Well, obviously there's an interpretation and an analysis and prioritization process that sits behind how you decide to put into operations the kinds of levels of services you would want based on what you've both found from the information you've received and the analysis of it.

Seems to be quite a challenge, and of course we could just decide to pick, "Oh well, here's a very troublesome area." That's how political process for allocating health resources might work. "Here's a very challenging area. Let's put funding there." But- Mm-hmm do you think we need a working draft of a model in the absence ofOf one that comes to mind to give a sense that here's a reasonable service expectation.

That's helpful. Because a lot of us engage in advocacy of different sorts around health services around this table. And I am greatly assisted by the idea that, to use the maternity example again, every woman giving birth in New Zealand should have access to full antenatal, postpartum, and intrapartum care.

That seems to be a very powerful concept for advocating for services. How are we going to get by without this concept? I think without trying to sound political about this, I think a long-term view of what is important is part of a, I'm talking cross-party, bipartisan approach. Looking long-term, I think is really important around how those decisions are made.

Because there's obviously, if this is the policy, the decisions that need to be made, there's an operationalization of those decisions. I think having some consistency and long-term view about what good could look like is important. Probably that's as far as I could go. Possibly. Those of us who are politicians in the room might feel that those sorts of agreements are aided by independent advice on what- Yeah an appropriate level of service provision is.

Mm-hmm. Yeah. I can only... Sorry. Did you want to make a comment? Sorry. I would like to hear from Barbara. I've been around a long time, as some of you will know. And the time that I felt we began to get a bit of a grip was when there was the Mental Health Blueprint, and that was really rough and ready when I look back now.

But it was really around at the time, looking at what was the best guess in terms of epidemiology, getting people in a room who knew what the impacts were because they either had experience, or they were clinicians, or they were family. And really starting to think long-term about what are the components of a system that need to be there, and what do people need over the course of episodes of illness to really support them where they are through to a space and place.

And then to be able to conceptualize what that is, what it's going to cost, and what you need to do incrementally to begin to build the system forward. Thank you. Over to you, Sam. Thank you. And I think there's been a very interesting discussion about kind of need to what specialist services.

That, of course, is one part of the equation. The next part of the equation is the outcomes from the specialist service, whatever shape they form. Do we have actually measures to actually indicate that? Because that would be the final piece of the model. Yeah. There are measures across the system now that much more are looking at the clinical outcomes for people and how the difference that has made, the access and choice information does take measures on that.

What we probably need to do a lot more and have more systemic across our system is what do individuals who experience a service actually think about it, and to collect much better data on what they- Experience... yes, what they experienced and what might have helped them- Exactly... in that whole area. So that's not collected universally across our system at all points, although we do collect clinical indicators.

Mm-hmm. And I suppose that's kind of going back end of the model, but actually coming back the other way, inch. Yep. What work is going on in the prevention space? There is work underway in the early intervention, prevention space. Absolutely. So, trying to understand what are those factors that are contributing to distress and how some of those could be addressed.

A very topical one is around online safety. But so how do you understand what those factors are and what can be done to prevent the distress occurring? And then when people are starting to experience distress, how the early intervention services and supports are available for people, and how people can keep themselves well. And that is about looking across the system. There's various agencies have various roles to play across that part of the system. But certainly, that's an area where more emphasis needs to be put into what's

available. One of the areas that needs further work is around digital or e-mental health. What are the sort of digital tools that are available for people to support them early? So it is a lot that is underway, but also further work that needs to happen in that area. There is more work that could be done or should be done in the primary and community areas of service, and that I think frontline staff are reporting.

People are presenting in a much higher acuity when opportunities were lost further on and possible intervention occurring earlier. So yeah, there are actual service gaps in some regions. Some areas are better. So even just getting the lumpiness out of the system and more consistency would go some way in terms of providing people with more choices before becoming more highly acute.

Mm-hmm. We do know that the- The impact in the early years of young people's lives, the impact of violence, the impact of poverty, falling out of school early. There is lots and lots of evidence about the long-term preventative approaches one can take in social, cultural, education domains that really do make a difference. Removing violence from young people's lives, removing abuse from young people's lives, giving every child a fair opportunity are the best preventative responses that we can take.

Mr. Tur? We've got to subset and group. Yeah. Oh, no, sorry. No. You've answered it. Just- Four months ago. No, it's all right. Thank you. Dr. Disley, the blueprint you were referring to- Mm-hmm... that's the one published in 1997? Mm-hmm. 1998. Wow.

So my question really does pick up on this, and what I see is perhaps the gap that we've got. We've talked about the incidents of distress, and distress is at that end where you're mentioning social determinants, we've mentioned prevention, and yet when we look at mapping it onto what is being measured, it is clinical responses. So there is a gap, isn't there, in terms of how we consider mental health and wellbeing- Welcome... or I would say mental and social health, which includes social infrastructure measurement versus clinical responses.

From a systems level- Yeah... does it make sense and is there a role for mental health to have an insights and data faculty to be able to inform better policy across government so that we don't perform in these tyrannies of silo? Do you? Yeah. This commission was set up intentionally with that scope across government.

So we're not solely within the health system, so we certainly have that reach and our powers to obtain information and to make recommendations are across government for that very reason. But we have needed to really focus our work on the areas that are most important.

And so that's what we have done, given our capacity and capability. Absolutely, and thinking outside of your organization because you have an independent watchdog role. But from a systems organizational perspective, will we be able to turn the dial more on prevention if we're not just looking at clinical, even the articulation of online safety. We can apply the same logic to housing- Mm... to poverty, to all sorts of social infrastructure. Have we got the approach too narrow because we're defining the problem very broadly, and then

our focus on outcomes is always from a clinical perspective. How do we reconcile that, and is there an opportunity for us to actually get a real change in prevention if we were to reorganize? Yeah. I would say our work, and particularly through our wellbeing outcome framework- Yeah... does focus on those broader outcomes for, wellbeing outcomes for people. And we have a connection then into our service level sort of framework. So we are keeping very much that wider wellbeing view, because there's no question about that.

We're just limited by how much we can do in that space, because we've had to prioritize to the level we have. But it's really important to us that we do keep that focus, and the work that we are doing is about the wellbeing outcomes for people who are interacting with mental health services- Mm rather than a broader sort of wellbeing.

If you were resourced more in a different way, you might be able to actually influence policy that would influence housing outcomes- Yes... and mental health outcomes and so on. But that's currently not available because of the scope, correct? Yeah. And the prioritization decisions we've needed to make- Yeah to fulfill our legislative mandate and our strategy and what we've heard from our key stakeholders about what's important.

Thank you. I'll go to Jenny, and then you've got to come. Okay. Jenny. Thank you. Influence. Really keen to understand when you talk about the position you have to influence, what are some material gains that you've made that you could articulate to the committee?

Okay. Yeah. I'll speak to the ones that are with the case studies we've highlighted in our annual report. Mm-hmm. So we can say that the mental health and wellbeing strategy, having a dedicated strategy that informs the work of all of government is really important.

That's something that we advocated for in the Pae Ora Bill. It wasn't included at that point in time, but the current Minister of Mental Health picked that up, and we now have a strategy that's out for consultation. That's really important to give that sort of focus and direction and see the investment behind that as well. And the- And what would that investment look like?

Are we talking dollar investment, or are we talking- Once the strategy is completed, then it's what sits behind that to make that happen. The other area of influence would be on the Access and Choice program. Oh, yeah. So we've monitored that program with three reports over the course of our five years. In the first report, we really highlighted the need to strengthen the focus on the kaupapa Māori, Pacific, and youth streams of that work, and we saw a response to that advice. And we've also seen with our final report, again, the minister has asked for an action

plan to respond to our recommendations to really see that program reach its full sort of achieving. We're seeing 325,000 people per annum. So we can see some areas quite specifically where-Our work has made a tangible difference. The other case study we highlight in our annual report is around the Mental Health Bill, and so the repeal and replace of the Mental Health Bill that was recommended in He Ara Oranga report of the inquiry back in 2018. We still haven't seen that bill come through to fruition.

We think that's a really important area to have focus, that we have got modern legislation that upholds people's human rights. There's certainly changes that can be made in practice, but that's another area that we've had a really sustained focus over the course of our existence. So would you advocate, and this is just a supplementary on what my colleague Cameron was asking around the priority populations, and that you're focused on those, and then focused on lived experience.

Are you bringing in the rural, the male, in terms of lived experience, so they become a priority population? I mean, is everyone not a priority population if you have some mental health challenges? And I suppose finally, we want to know, would you suggest removing those populations so that we don't have a siloed approach to who's more deserving?

I would say, I mean, we have particular commitments to Maori and Whanau, which is important, and we have a legislative function to advocate for the collective interests of people with lived experience and the people who support them. So those are really, really important priorities for us in terms of populations.

The other priority populations are the populations who are more likely to experience distress, and so what our focus is on is those people from those populations who are accessing or wanting to or needing to access services. So drawing from across those populations for that particular focus.

But it leaves gaps. It leaves gaps in our community, though, priority populations. Yes, it does. And I think that's the kind of... I'd like to know what you would recommend about how do you deal with those gaps in population that are of equal need? And so that's come through our assessment of wellbeing of all people in Aotearoa, New Zealand, and how we understand what some of those gaps might be. I think, as we've talked about, the challenges is the breadth of and complexity of those issues in those populations.

And so how, as a small entity, when we're looking at something like our system performance report, how do we bring those voices through into that report? So would you be advocating to expand your small entity to make it a slightly larger entity so you can bring those in? I mean, what are you recommending? Well, as I said before, we've become comfortable in our skin in knowing what we've got, and knowing what our purpose is. So I guess what we've done is we've compromised on what we might have thought was the potential of the commission from the outset.

So we're working, by necessity, we've gone back to focusing more on people with lived experience who are interacting with services and systems as a priority and looking at priority groups that might intersect through that lens, as opposed to those specific population groups through a population health lens, if that makes sense. Hopefully.

So what you're saying is you're capturing those groups that are not priority populations through their lived experience. Yes. Can I just sup on that and then have another sup? What does your data tell you about equity and outcomes for Maori and other of those prioritized groups in mental health compared to the general population?

Well, we know Maori experience higher levels of distress in other indicators, like higher levels of suicide for young Maori men. And so, this information is not new to us in the system. We've known it for some time. And might that explain why it's the legislative prioritization?

Well, that comes back to the legislators. Yeah. Yep. My second question is just picking up on the Mental Health Bill, and I know- This is a primary. We're going to go to Kahu's primary now. Can I just add an extra- Well, it was a sup on Jenny's question I'll come back. Because we keep getting bumped down. Sure. Because people jumping in with sups that are...

The first one was well-connected, and then that's how I'm going to judge it. Kahu. I think they had just one more thing to add. Oh, look, I was just going to add a comment in terms of the higher levels of need. We do see really high levels of need around Maori and Pacific peoples, and also for people who experience a disability and members of the rainbow community. So we are looking at what are some of those groups that we can see in the information are experiencing poorer wellbeing outcomes, and then you can look at how are those, where we can,

how are those people then being able to access services and support. So- Thank you. Thank you, Mr. Chair. I just want to talk about service performance and addressing the wider determinants of health and wellbeing. We know that young people's long-term wellbeing is intrinsically linked to their first 2,000 days and the health and wellbeing of their mama, parents, and wider whanau.

And too many, in fact, thousands of new mama, new parents, are clinically diagnosed with mental health disorder And that they are not able to access services until they are at a critical level in many cases. So when we're talking about maternal, perinatal, postnatal mental health, um, and how critical it is that these mama and parents are getting the services that they need.

I've got three supplementaries, Mr. Chair. What monitoring and advocacy are you working on around that maternal, peri/postnatal mental health space? We haven't got any specific activity in the maternal mental health area. So, we have prioritized access more generally, and then we prioritized crisis responses, um, and then prioritized, um, having a closer look at rangatahi and young people, so the sort of deep dive type reports that we do.

There's other areas that we know are really important, um, that we'd like to have a closer look at. Addiction services is one. We've had discussions around forensic services. Maternal mental health, um, would be one of those on that list, eating disorders. So there's a number of specific service areas that there would be benefit in having a closer look at.

We haven't done that um, to date. Thank you. And so we've talked about prevention here. I'm just wrapping my supplementary from the other one into this question here. Um, and around that advocacy to ministers and ministries, um, the prevention is such a huge part of this and will actually mean that we're not spending as much on those critical, um, mental health needs.

And then there'll be more availability, um, hopefully. Were you consulted or asked for advice from the Minister for Children or the Minister for Prevention, Family and Sexual Violence around cutting prevention funding, um, and services? So for example, women's centers being closed.

No. Not that I'm aware of. Thank you. Um, and I guess just to wrap up, we were looking at your, um, revenue expenses for '24/25, and they're looking pretty good, on par there. What are the implications of your funding that you receive currently, um, around what you are able to output and, um, advocate for around your, um, strategic priorities?

I think we've probably traversed a lot of those constraints already. Yeah. So, as I say, the strategic refresh that we undertook last year was in part to recognize there's the potential scope of what our legislation, um, says and then the actual resources. And so based on the experiences of the previous four years, uh, we were making priority decisions about where we could go with the resources that we've got, which is why we're prioritizing people with lived experience, prioritizing, um, focus on service, um,

experience, and contact. So yeah. And even within those- Yeah. And, oh. Oh, go ahead. Look, um, some of the areas that we're particularly constrained is around some of our, um, lived experience capability, some of our, um, engagement with thinking about the sort of priority populations, um, our data and analytical capability. Um, so there are some areas that we are particularly constrained, um, and that has factored into that sort of prioritization of our work.

And so even within those, um, strategic priorities and those priority populations that you're looking into, if you had more funding, you would be able to, um, go further in terms of advocacy, um, and addressing those wider determinants for mental health. Mm-hmm. Yes. And in that broader wellbeing space, um, absolutely and also looking at, um, the increasing drug use, um, and so substance-related harm and addiction services. That's an area that we've done some work, but not as much as what is needed.

So there's a number of areas that we've identified that, um, we believe we could add value to if we were able to, um, take a closer look at those areas. Um, we just haven't been able to. And the work that you've done in, um, addiction and, um, drug harm, what is it showing in terms of its linkage to mental health?

Oh look, there's definitely a connection between the two as well as, uh, separate issues. Um, so we have done work to look at, um, access to addiction services, um, which has increased in this last year, which is good. But there's really a need to look at what are the range and types of services that are available for people, um, and what would really make a difference.

So there's, like we did with crisis responses, having a look at what does it look like and what does the system look like now, and what are the changes that need to be made. I mean, that's work that we could do. Um, we're doing some work, a sort of evidence brief or literature scan around, um, substance harm, so that will also inform future work.

Mm-hmm. There, I just want to make a more broad statement in that, we are, um, about to enter into a formal review, which was built into the legislation, and it may be a good opportunity to have discussions of this nature. Thank you. Thank you. All right. Carlos.

Yes. I'm trying to better understand your role. Obviously, you provide some advice to the minister if it's required. But do you actually involve in making a submission or contributing to some of the new legislation? Yes. So is alcohol-related legislation a part of your responsibility?

When there's legislation bills going through the process, through select committee, then we do have our prioritization criteria. So we assess each of those bills in terms of the connection to the work that we're doing, how important it is for the communities, mostly for people with lived experience- Mm-hmm... and whether we've got something that we can add value to.

So that's what you've seen us a number of times presenting to you as- Mm-hmm... Health Select Committee on various legislation, particularly the Mental Health Bill and others. So we do choose or prioritize which bills we submit on because of the breadth of our role, we could submit on many bills, and in our early years we did. But that meant that that capacity wasn't available for other work.

So we have become more focused on what we submit on. So correct me if I'm wrong, I see there's no record of the commission actually make a submission on Sales and Supply of Alcohol Amendment Bill about the Easter egg business selling alcohol sales. Is there any reason why you guys are not putting a submission in?

Or will you guys go to put a submission on the future improving alcohol regulation bill? Oh, I can't specifically comment on that. I'll ask Anna if we can look for that. We made a submission. No, we didn't, did we? No, I think so, no. We can come back to you with information on that.

Yes. So sorry. When some of the legislation will be related to alcohol addiction, and you're trying to tell me that you're not making a submission yet. So I'm just a little bit concerned on your role as the advisor to our government. Yeah. Okay. All right.

Hon. Ingrid Leary. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Looking at the Mental Health Bill and looking at your strong advocacy in June 2022, and then again, your submission to the select committee regarding seclusion, you stated as a body that you wanted to see us go as far as possible to end seclusion.

And there wasn't agreement in this committee, and in fact, there's going to be instead of sunsetting of seclusion, there'll be a review in five years- Mm-hmm... not even in two years, which some members had asked for. I'm curious to know, also given that our Maori are at least four times more likely to experience seclusion, are you concerned about what that's going to mean for New Zealand meeting its international obligations?
Are you concerned about what it means for Maori, and do you see it as a missed opportunity?

We can live with the outcome of where the draft is now. What we would say is that practice needs to change and can change. It would be good to have the legislation behind, and we know that there are some places that have achieved zero seclusion within New Zealand. So it's not out of the realm of possibility. It's not an unrealistic goal.

So again, being pragmatic, we could live with the legislation. If there's a built-in review, all the better. But we can show that there is already changes, and there are good examples of where we can achieve zero or very close to it. If we had sunsetted seclusion in the bill, that would provide further incentives and further drive to end the practices and change the culture of seclusion.

Yeah. Would you agree with that? Supplementary. Is that the commission's position? Yes. Even if the government were to change? I'm not sure what that means. Will that be the commission's position even if the government- Oh... is to change, that the legislation should stay with seclusion being permitted?

Yes. That would be our position, yeah. Our preference is that there is a sunset clause. We've been clear on that over the five years of our advocacy around the Mental Health Bill. I think what Hayden said was that we can live with it because there are many changes that can be made in practice, and there are some inpatient units that have eliminated seclusion.

So it is achievable regardless of whether there is legislation or not. The legislation helps, there's no question about that. But this is about what happens on the ground as well. So what would be those changes that should happen on the ground now, given that the legislation, we continue to- Well, I guess what we're saying is it's possible for those changes to take place without having the sunset clause built into it.

But it would obviously help, I would think, as a lever for change if the legislation was there. How big a priority should this issue be in mental health given that you're suggesting operational changes now are the option that remains, given that the government won't change the law?

How much should they be prioritized so that people experiencing mental health crises have their human rights upheld? So we're here to amplify the voices of people with lived experience, and they're very clear in terms of what they are seeking. So that's our position in terms of supporting that view.

Just wanted to give you another chance to state what your position is on seclusion. Look, through a human rights lens, having the ability to be involved in the decisions, having support for consent, and so on is important. So, our position hasn't changed on that.

Okay. So you haven't modified your position-... based on the government's position? No, we haven't modified our position at all. Natural answers. But what we have said is that we will work, if we can, with the sector to influence the changes that we think can happen. In fact, it's a good idea, and we've put it back to the sector and said to the sector itself, "You can do it.

We know what success looks like. You know what success looks like. How can you replicate that across other parts of New Zealand?" But it's your preference to have the bill- Sorry. Well, that's our position. That's your position. That's our stated position. Yeah. Hamish. Excellent. Primary. Thank you. You work in a space of, I suppose, a rich ecosystem is one way you'd probably describe it.

You've got different levels of operation, right, from probably the statutory oversight kind of function, through to kind of the advocacy function, where you probably share with other organizations, maybe some NGOs, and then probably kind of an operational lever pulling, if I could use a colloquial term. So how do you fit in with organizations, maybe if we start at the statutory oversight bodies like the Health and Disability Commission, the Health Quality & Safety Commission, the Ministry of Health.

Yeah, there's probably some others that are probably missing from this. How do you interact with those organizations- Yeah... to deliver the better outcomes? I can speak a little bit to that. So, with the Ministry of Health, the Ministry of Health is both our sort of monitoring agency, and we also have a close relationship in terms of oversight of mental health and addiction a-across the system.

So we both have respective monitoring roles. But there are key differences in those monitoring roles, particularly with the commission having independent functions to monitor the mental health and addiction system, and also across government. In terms of some of the other entities, Health and Disability Commissioner is an independent Crown entity, like this commission, focused on complaints, so individual situations, whereas this commission doesn't have a role with individuals. It's around collective interests of groups. So we do connect closely

with the Health and Disability Commission for those collective insights, because they're also responding to complaints about mental health and addiction services. The Health Quality and Safety Commission is also a Crown entity. Crown agents are closer to government with a role around leading and coordinating quality improvement. They have some monitoring roles as well.

And that entity did run the National Mental Health and Addiction Quality Improvement Program. We've had a stream about aiming for zero seclusion. So we have worked closely with the Health Quality and Safety Commission around those sort of joint areas of interest. Health Quality and Safety Commission also has the sort of sector lead around consumer invoice and consumer engagement. We have our own internal capability around lived experience, so there's connection points there.

So it's understanding the nature of the different agencies and our areas of common interest, and then having those relationships so that we keep connected with those entities. We're a member of what's called the Quality Forum. So that brings together all of the health entities, including the Cancer Control Agency and others, into a collective oversight or information sharing about what's happening from a quality perspective.

So we keep a lot of those and hold those government relationships in collaboration. Excellent. Yeah, and I suppose from our point of view, it's hard to separate out where the duplication is- It is... and where separate lanes are. Yeah. When it comes to the advocacy space, who do you see your other like-minded organizations being?

Yeah. I think we probably have more duplication. That's an overview question around the monitoring space. Mm. There are probably more of us monitoring. And I was going to say there needs to be, but there is definitely an opportunity for us to be tighter around how we coordinate who's doing what in the monitoring space.

And that is an active discussion that we have with both the Ministry of Health, and Health New Zealand. And in terms of the advocacy organizations, it depends on the particular area of focus. So for example, in relation to our work around young people, we do have a close relationship with Mana Mokopuna and the Children's Commissioner in that area.

We'd also keep connected with other entities, such as the Mental Health Foundation, that also have an advocacy role. So it's really looking at what is the particular area of focus and who are the agencies that are also in that particular area, and how we keep connected. Some of those entities like Mana Mokopuna have good connections into young people, and we can draw on some of their insights and knowledge without having to do all of that work ourselves.

So it's really saying, "Well, which entities? What's the topic, and who do we work with?" And some entities pick up our work as well. Absolutely. Mm. Yes. And I suppose last up for each, yeah. And I suppose the last kind of level, both the Ministry of Health holdsIn effect, and I probably have very crudely called it pulling the levers for Health New Zealand to change.

Mm-hmm. How do you separate out your functions in that role as well? And you might have probably a more technical term for that function. Yeah. I think most of the action really has been sharing where we think changes need to take place and at an operational level, understanding that we've got a separation between governance and operations, most of that activity occurs at an operational level, and it's often leader to leader.

And quite firm advocacy discussions are held behind closed doors. Mm. And so is that with the Ministry of Health or with Health New Zealand? Both. Yeah. We have regular engagement with the Ministry of Health, again, on the particular areas of focus that they might be working on and that we are.

And then also with Health New Zealand and different parts of Health New Zealand. And certainly those connections have strengthened in recent times. So the connections around specialist services and then also their commissioning function and into NGOs. So, it's quite broad engagement depending on what we're working on and with our particular interests. So for seclusion, for example, it's connecting more to the specialist part of Health New Zealand.

Yeah. Thanks for that. We've got two primaries. One from Ingrid and then Carlos. Thank you. I'm interested to know, you've talked about being in touch with a lot of different parts of the sector, and you did mention with seclusion, talking to Health New Zealand, I'm thinking also about the Mental Health Foundation and interested to know what their position would be.

But regarding workforce, I've been talking to NCAT, to the platform and I think the characterization would be that we've gone from a workforce that had in a redacted document at least 1,500 vacancies in mental health. That was redacted out by Lester Levy.

But we know that there have been slowly filling up of some of those spaces- Yeah... which is a move in the right direction. Mm-hmm. But what work are you doing to track retention? Because I'm thinking here about a workforce that's telling me it's highly feminized, it's burned out. We've seen lots of people go to Australia. They've had pay equity cuts. It's one thing to fill jobs, it's another thing to retain them.

What is your characterization of the workforce and what work are you doing to monitor retention and pathways to make sure that we have enough workers to service the sector? Probably not to the degree that you're referring to. And I would see that as an important responsibility for an employer to be considering in terms of their own workforce.

We can shine the light on where service gaps occur, and where they arise as a result of vacancies. And you pointed out a very good example, where we've seen access to specialist services start to lift because of vacancies being filled. But we wouldn't probably exert a lot of energy or effort into that space ourselves other than showing where we might see service gaps occurring because of it.

It's difficult, though, if somebody's doing the same job in, say, the community sector and the funding uplift has not been the same as for direct employment from Health New Zealand. So you can say to employers you need to pay more, but if the settings- Yeah... aren't there from a policy perspective, that's not going to happen, is it? Yeah.

So is that a problem? It is a problem. And- And the NGO sector will tell you so. And there's a real lack of good workforce information across the sector, particularly in the NGO sector. Mm-hmm. We did some early work looking at the proportion of the peer support workforce, in both Health New Zealand and NGOs.

But the information about workforce and NGOs is just not frequent enough and not granular enough, and certainly an area that needs to improve. It's a problem having a bit of a divided sector, isn't it? Because you've got people doing the same jobs but on different pay rates. You've got paucity of information.

Yeah. From a systems level, is that something that you think is impacting health outcomes? In our broad view of what solutions look like, we've got a strong focus on communities being part of the solution. So the NGO sector, community sector is a really important part, and particularly if we're talking about early intervention, prevention, and so forth.

Thank you. Finally, just on psychiatry, youth psychiatry in particular. Mm-hmm. I'm seeing another niche part, but very critical part of the sector in crisis. In fact, even within the Ministry's ranks and Health New Zealand's ranks, I think that we've seen workforce gaps there. Are you satisfied that the government's doing enough to resolve that high acuity end of the workforce matrix? And what would you like to see happen around workforce planning for that?

Well, we know it's imperfect. Frontline staff are telling us that people are presenting often with very high levels of acuity, where interventions could have occurred earlier. So yeah, that is a concern. How we're addressing it is really basically through our own interactions with leadership.

Which leadership? Health New Zealand. And so, our role is to really highlight where the gaps are and the areas are for improvement. Yeah. It's then for Health New Zealand to determine, and other agencies to determine what the solutions are and to act on those. Are they acting on what you're saying?

LookWe have seen response to our advice, absolutely, in terms of, and those engagements that we have particularly around the recent access infographic that we released. And so that's bringing some new insights. And so there is a process to understand what is driving some of what we're seeing, and then what are the actions that will actually make a difference.

So we are seeing the system shift, and we are seeing more responsiveness. But we've still got a long way to go. I think with some of the power of what we bring is that cross-system view. So more than just one entity. It's looking right across the system. And that's the space that we really think we can add value.

Bearing in mind that our monitoring reports, we're only just beginning to follow up on some of our earlier advice. So as I said, this was one of the first pieces of work that we've actually gone back to revisit and say, "If you'd like, put a scorecard on it. How did you do?" What, three out of the five recommendations that we made have been picked up. Two are starting to be worked on now.
