So it's early on in our cycle of following through on the advice that we've originally given. I'm trying to find the reference, but I can't. But I read recently from another sector group that one in five, maybe it was one in four, consultant psychiatrist positions in Health New Zealand are vacant.

Is that correct? I don't know. That's about a 20% vacancy rate. Sounds about right. We've reported on that in our reports, yes. And that would be up to the end of June '25. Can you explain further what impact your raising that in reports has had on Health New Zealand's behavior?

I couldn't talk specifically to Health New Zealand's behavior. There's certainly an understanding of those high rates of vacancies and the impact that that has had on the ability for people to access services. And so we have seen, particularly with the filling of nurses and other vacancy rates in that '24, '25 year, that has resulted in more people being able to access those specialist services. And that's been in conversation with Health New Zealand.

But we're still concerned about the level of psychiatry vacancies. Yeah. So that is, what are the actions that are being taken through the workforce plan and other operational activities that Health New Zealand undertake. So, sorry to interrupt. I think that their workforce plan was something that we put in as a recommendation for them to undertake, so that they've got a longer-term view. And we've tied it in.

It's not just about filling vacancies, but it's also there's a real need to tie it in with different ways of doing things. What are the models of delivery? What are the models of care that go with it? And I think those two conversations need to be hand in hand. Indeed. There are vacancies in other areas of the health system too.

Yeah. How would you advise? Can you give me any information that helps me understand the priority that should be given to psychiatrist vacancy? I can't give you specifics. But I do understand that it's an international issue, as it is for other specialties.

So we're often competing on an international stage. There is probably an argument, which we haven't specifically had, but there is probably an argument for more home ground specialist psychiatrists. Sorry, perhaps I should just clarify. Do you see safety risks around this level of vacancy?

Do you see the main implication as people not being able to receive care in a timely way but ultimately get it and become well again? How do we assess the impact of that level of vacancy? Because of course, there are hard decisions to make about where resources are put.

And we certainly hear a lot through our engagement about the impact of people not being able to access services in a timely way. So people then presenting with a higher level of need, more complex needs that take a longer timeframe in terms of recovery for people and their whanau.

Impacts on whanau are significant as well, particularly rangatahi and youth. We also hear about the impacts on primary and community services, who tell us about how they're supporting people with higher levels of need for longer, and that's continuing to increase.

So that has impacts both for people and also for those other services. And when you look across the system and say, "What is those impacts around less access to specialist services?" It does flow right through the system and into communities. So we definitely hear and see that in our system monitoring work.

And it may, in fact, have an impact on why we have some challenges around the use of coercive practices. Yeah. That's what I was going to say. High use of compulsory treatment orders- Mm-hmm... and the ability to reduce seclusion- Mm-hmm... are dependent upon having people in place who can apply their skills well.

Mm-hmm. So isn't one of the issues that- Many of our psychiatrists have gone to Australia. We're competing with Australia from the same pool of countries, and we may not have the infrastructure regarding supervision for psychiatry or even psychology within our universities in a planned and in a pipeline way to be able to service our needs. And what advice have you given, and are you satisfied with the response? Because these are really crunchy issues that are impacting, I would argue, even our suicide rates, and they're urgent. So

is that the problem, and what needs to be done? It is an-- It has been on our advice, indeed, and we do see it as important. And we don't see it as just a filling the vacancy issue. When you talk about pipeline, we're talking about a medium to long-term view before we're going to see the results. And so it requires a sustained, clear plan, which is why we put that to Health New Zealand in 2024.

25. 24. So that's something we've seen as a limiting factor on a number of solutions. And all that goes with it, the whole, the ecosystem around workforce development, support, supervision, ongoing. It's, yeah. We also sort of heard a lot about it in terms of what happens for particular clinical teams. So if there's a high turnover rate or more senior and experienced staff move on, then the team can carry a lower caseload, need to carry a lower caseload.

They don't have as much time in terms of, well, they need to spend more time supporting or coaching new people coming in. There's dynamics around use of locums. So all of that dynamic within a team means that it has lesser sort of capacity and some capability in terms of the people that they're then able to see.

So there is a dynamic that happens in there, and it does absolutely need to be addressed as part of that wider picture. It's also thinking about what are the other roles and how does the team work together. So we're certainly a supporter of growing the peer support workforce. We know that that workforce can make a real difference for people as well in terms of supporting people through their journey.

So, and I would add, as well as the sort of cultural workforce, so services that will meet people's full needs, be that for Maori, Pacific, for Asian, whatever the population is. Thank you. We've heard about task shifting on the committee previously. Mr. Chair, just final sup. So to clarify, we have heard an explanation that psychiatrist vacancies directly and indirectly impact timely access to services. We've had an explanation that that may have negative impacts on the human rights aspects of care, of use of seclusion and compulsory
treatment orders. But does the commission have concerns about clinical safety as a result of this level of shortage?

Can you just elaborate on clinical safety? Well, unsafe mental health care can lead to people being unsafe in mental health units or immediately on discharge, and in some cases, that can have disastrous consequences for those people. I'm trying to understand your level of concern about the understaffing and mental health care.

Well, as I said, we've got to the point that it became a recommendation on our part to see a way for-- or to encourage a way forward for that- For the reasons stated about human rights and access to services or safety. Yeah. Or clinical safety. Is anybody able to respond to that question?

Look, we don't look at particular aspects of clinical safety. I mean, the safety aspect that we have a focus on is around use of seclusion and sort of coercive practices. There's limited information of the experience of people using services. The agency that has the sort of the quality and safety mandate is HQEC. So it's a conversation that we have with HQEC to understand, in HDC, what might some of those clinical safety concerns have been raised.

So you do have that conversation? We do through the quality forum. So some of those conversations are early conversations. I think, yeah, this is something that's worth us having a closer look at. But finally- I think I'm taking away from this a question about clinical governance. But- And also at the beginning, I asked about accountability in the health system, and you've now mentioned other organizations.

And equally, when we talk to them, they mention you. Yes. Doesn't this highlight- Yeah... the leadership gap and how we manage to shift the dial on some of these critical issues so that it becomes more than a conversation or more than recommendations? Mm-hmm. Yeah. Agree. All right. This is our last round.

Just trying to do a little bit of positive. We're talking about vacancy rate just now. We look at the data, we know that the vacancy rate is peaking around 2022 to '23 at over 11%, but actually saw the sign of improvement in the late of 2024 and '25- Yeah... to just under 10%. I think there's a huge improvement.

Mm-hmm. But what I want to say is, because we also know that one of the target you achieve is to train more than 500 professional, and I think you achieved that target. That's the minister's target, I believe. Yes. So for those over 500 professional being trained, who are they? What role are they? Are they psychologists or support worker?

And how that actually improve the vacancy rate in the future? Anybody going to handle on it? Not specifically. I mean, it's the minister's target. It's the minister's action plan through Health New Zealand. Mm-hmm. So we've obviously aware of it- Mm-hmm... but I haven't, yeah, I couldn't comment at this point in time on the breakdown of those workforces.

Okay. That information is available. Sure. Yeah, I just haven't got it in my head. Thank you. Mr. Chair, if I may, before we close off, I just think it'd be important that we acknowledge Hayden Wano. I think it might be your last time before the committee in your role, Mental Health Commission.

So- Yeah... after six or seven years of tireless work, I just think it's important that we acknowledge your contribution and- Thank you anyway. Well done, Ingrid. Thank you very much. Yes. Thank you to all of you for presenting in front of us.
